headline: Bestinet ran Malaysia's visa system for RM400M — no contract, Interpol arrest request
context: Bloomberg's 10-month investigation found Bestinet founder Aminul Islam's firms made over RM400M from Malaysia's migrant visa system. Workers pay up to RM25,000 in recruitment fees. MACC closed its probe in September 2024; Cabinet extended the contract to 2031.

cards.big: One firm runs Malaysia's migrant visa system. Its founder faces an Interpol arrest request. The government just extended his contract to 2031.
cards.sub: Bloomberg found the founder pocketed RM400M. Workers pay RM25,000 each. MACC closed its probe in September 2024 — Cabinet extended the deal the month before.

cards.big: Six years with no signed contract. Then the per-worker fee was set at RM215 — more than double the government's own RM86 rate.
cards.sub: PAC found 24 unauthorised users approving employer applications. The government had no access to source code. Handover is now required before 2031.

cards.big: Bangladesh sought the founder's arrest via Interpol. Malaysia asked Bangladesh to drop its trafficking probes to protect its TIP ranking.
cards.sub: 29% of migrant domestic workers — mostly women excluded from the Employment Act — meet ILO forced-labour indicators. Workers pay permit fees that employers should.

cards.big: Zahid created the 10-agency restriction. Anwar extended the contract despite his own research centre's warning. A 2023 open tender drew just two bidders.
cards.sub: Visas now process in 14 days, down from 120. Every coalition has left the system in place because dismantling it would disrupt labour supply.

cards.big: Malaysian businesses across all communities profit from bonded cheap labour while working-class Malaysians bear the wage suppression.
cards.sub: US$1 billion has been siphoned from Bangladesh. Sabah and Sarawak run a separate crisis with 1 million undocumented migrants outside this system entirely.

cards.big: Like outsourcing passport control to a toll-booth operator who cut wait times — then learning the operator charges five times the rate and has no contract.
cards.sub: Speed built on worker debt bondage is not efficiency. It is exploitation with a dashboard.

cards.big: The fix exists — open tendering, employer-pays enforcement, cooperation with Bangladesh. No coalition will accept the disruption.
